title: Cross-Border Insolvency & Emerging Issues
content:
Session 10: Emerging issues in corporate insolvency law 
STO v Rainbow Papers [2022 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč In addition to private creditors, the CD owed substantial statutory dues to the State under the GVAT Act (unpaid taxes) ‚Üí 
STO filed claims for unpaid VAT dues. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč BUT, RP rejected/waived off the government‚Äôs claim ‚Üí treated it as an OC with no security interest.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč The approved Resolution Plan gave zero allocation to government dues. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Government‚Äôs Argument ‚Üí under Section 48, Gujarat VAT Act: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A ‚Äúfirst charge‚ÄĚ is created in favour of the State on the property of the dealer ‚Üí gov is a secured creditor 
under IBC. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT ‚Äď Held in favour of the government. Recognised charge under Section 48 GVAT as creating security interest. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLAT ‚Äď set aside NCLT. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 53 IBC overrides Section 48 GVAT ‚Üí gov is merely an operational creditor, placed low in waterfall 
(s.53(1)(e)). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Delay in filing claim ‚Üí valid ground for rejecting it. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No ‚Äúsecurity interest‚ÄĚ existed. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Are the IBC timelines for submission of claims mandatory or directory? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Does Section 48 of the GVAT Act create a ‚Äúsecurity interest‚ÄĚ, making the State a secured creditor under s.3(30), s.3(31) and 
s.53(1)(b)(ii) IBC? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Was the AA (NCLT) right in approving a Resolution Plan that gave zero dues to the Government? 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.3(30) ‚ÄĒ Secured Creditor in whose favour security interest is created ‚Üí no exclusion of government. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.3(31) ‚ÄĒ Security Interest 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Includes mortgage, charge, hypothecation, assignment, encumbrance. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Includes security interest created by operation of law. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Excludes only performance guarantees. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.53(1)(b)(ii) ‚ÄĒ Waterfall 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Dues of secured creditors (who relinquished security interest). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Rank pari passu with workmen‚Äôs dues for preceding 24 months. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.30(2)(b) ‚ÄĒ Mandatory contents of resolution plan 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Must provide at least liquidation value to operational creditors. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Cannot pay them less than they would get under Section 53. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.31(1) ‚ÄĒ AA‚Äôs duty 
‚ěĘ‚Äč AA must be ‚Äúsatisfied‚ÄĚ that plan complies with s.30(2). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Not an empty formality. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč GVAT Act, s.48 ‚Üí creates a statutory first charge in favour of the State over the dealer's property. 
73 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Judicial Precedents Referred 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Ebix v Educomp (2021) ‚ÄĒ  
‚Ė†‚Äč
The resolution plan must conform to statutory requirements. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Interpretation principle: ‚ÄúMay‚ÄĚ can be read as ‚Äúshall‚ÄĚ if required. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Regulation 38(1) of CIRP Regulations 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Dissenting creditors must be paid before assenting creditors. 
 
Application:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 1: Are IBC timelines mandatory or directory? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Timelines for filing claims are directory, not mandatory. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Delay cannot extinguish a statutory right. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč RP should have called for or verified government claims. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, RP‚Äôs rejection of STO‚Äôs claim was illegal. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 2: Is the State a secured creditor under s.53(1)(b)(ii)? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Does s.48 GVAT create a ‚Äúsecurity interest‚ÄĚ? 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 48 creates a statutory first charge ‚Üí a form of security interest. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Security interest under s.3(31) can arise ‚Äúby operation of law‚ÄĚ. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Nothing in IBC excludes State from being a secured creditor. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No conflict between IBC s.53 and GVAT s.48 
‚Ė†‚Äč
s.53(1)(b)(ii) expressly includes debts owed to secured creditors, which can include government. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Since IBC does not override or contradict GVAT here, s.48 stands. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Government is operational creditor, but can be secured 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The government is an operational creditor ‚Üí BUT, OC can also be secured ‚áí due to S.48 ‚Üí state is a 
secured creditor. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, the State qualifies as a secured creditor under s.53(1)(b)(ii). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 3: Was approval of a resolution plan that gave zero to the govt valid? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Resolution Plan must comply with s.30(2)  ‚Üí RP and CoC must ensure: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Statutory creditors get liquidation value (at minimum) ‚Üí cannot give them zero. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Ignoring statutory dues is illegal. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
A plan providing no allocation to statutory creditors must be rejected. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
If a company cannot pay statutory dues ‚Üí liquidation should follow. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč AA‚Äôs role under s.31 is substantive 
‚Ė†‚Äč
AA must be truly ‚Äúsatisfied‚ÄĚ ‚Üí cannot treat approval as mechanical or ‚Äúempty formality‚ÄĚ. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
‚ÄúMay‚ÄĚ in s.31(2) reads as ‚Äúshall‚ÄĚ when the plan is not compliant. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Consequence 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Entire Resolution Plan set aside. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
RP must consider a fresh plan. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Resolution Applicants can resubmit with provisions for statutory dues. 
 
Conclusion 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Timelines for filing claims are directory, not mandatory. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč RP cannot reject government dues only because of delay. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč State is a secured creditor due to statutory charge under s.48 GVAT, which creates a security interest by operation of law 
under s.3(31) IBC. 
 
Paschimanchal Vidyut v Raman  Ispat [2023 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CD procured electricity from PVVNL under an agreement. 
74 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Clause 5: Outstanding electricity dues would be a charge on the assets; the property could be attached until dues 
were cleared. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CD defaulted ‚Üí PVVNL attached property in 2016 through Tehsildar. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CIRP failed ‚Üí liquidation ordered. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Liquidator sought release of attached property ‚Üí argued PVVNL must participate in liquidation and take 
proceeds as per s.53. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT & NCLAT agreed ‚Üí attachment lifted ‚Üí PVVNL treated as an operational creditor (with a charge). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč PVVNL appealed, relying heavily on Rainbow Papers. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether PVVNL is a secured creditor by virtue of contractual charge under the Electricity Supply Code. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether PVVNL‚Äôs dues qualify as government dues under s.53(1)(e). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the Electricity Act overrides the IBC (given its own non-obstante clauses). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether Rainbow Papers applies. 
 
Rule: 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč PVVNL is a Secured Creditor 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Agreement + Clause 4.3(f)(iv) of the Electricity Supply Code validly created a contractual charge ‚Üí NCLAT had 
correctly held PVVNL to be a secured operational creditor, but still subject to the IBC framework. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč PVVNL is Not ‚ÄúGovernment‚ÄĚ Under s.53(1)(e) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč ‚ÄúGovernment dues‚ÄĚ are taxes, tariffs, amounts payable into the Consolidated Fund (Art. 265). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
PVVNL, though government-owned, is a statutory corporation whose dues do not enter the 
Consolidated Fund. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Many private entities can also be electricity suppliers under the 2003 Act ‚Üí dues cannot automatically be 
‚Äúgovernment dues.‚ÄĚ 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Thus, PVVNL‚Äôs dues ‚Č† s.53(1)(e) (government) 
‚óŹ‚Äč
They are dues of a secured creditor under s.53(1)(b) or s.53(1)(e)(ii) depending on 
relinquishment. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč IBC Overrides the Electricity Act 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Even though the Electricity Act has non-obstante clauses (ss.173, 174): 
‚Ė†‚Äč
s.238 IBC = special statute for insolvency and distribution of assets 
‚ěĘ‚Äč So, attachment orders under the Electricity Act cannot block liquidation or asset sale. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč How Sections 52 and 53 Work 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Secured Creditor Has Two Choices: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Relinquish security ‚Üí asset becomes part of liquidation estate ‚Üí priority under s.53(1)(b). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Enforce security independently under s.52 ‚Üí 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Must first notify liquidator in Form C/D 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Excess realised ‚Üí must be returned to liquidator (s.52(7)) 
‚óŹ‚Äč
If recovery insufficient ‚Üí shortfall ranks lower under s.53(1)(e)(ii). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Hence, secured creditors take a ‚Äúcalculated decision‚ÄĚ: high priority if they relinquish; low if they enforce 
independently. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
PVVNL never followed s.52 ‚Üí cannot claim independent enforcement ‚Üí must fall back into s.53. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Why Rainbow Papers Does Not Apply 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SC distinguishes sharply: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Rainbow = Resolution Stage 
‚Ė†‚Äč
This case = Liquidation stage. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 53 applies only in liquidation ‚Üí Rainbow never considered Section 53. 
75 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Rainbow never analysed the waterfall mechanism ‚Üí mistakenly treated state tax dues as ‚Äúsecured 
creditors‚ÄĚ under a state statute. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Completely missed that s.53 places government dues below even unsecured creditors. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Legislative Intent 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Parliament separated ‚Äúsecured creditors‚ÄĚ from ‚Äúgovernment dues.‚ÄĚ ‚Üí Treating government dues as secured defeats 
s.53. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč PVVNL is a secured creditor (secured operational creditor). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč PVVNL is not a government ‚Üí cannot claim higher priority under s.53(1)(e). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Attachment under the Electricity Act is overridden by s.238 IBC. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Property must be released to the liquidator. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč PVVNL must take its recovery strictly through s.53, depending on whether it relinquishes security. 
 
 
Sanjay Kumar Agarwal v STO [2023 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
In Rainbow Papers (2022), SC held that State tax authorities possessing a statutory first charge under Section 48 GVAT are secured 
creditors under s.3(30) IBC ‚Üí placed government dues higher in the waterfall (s.53(1)(b)(ii)), upsetting established expectations in 
insolvency practice. A later coordinate bench (equal strength), in PVVNL / Raman Ispat, expressed doubts, suggesting Rainbow 
Papers may have incorrectly interpreted Section 53 and the relative priority of government claims ‚áí review petition. 
 
Issues: Whether the SC‚Äôs decision in Rainbow Papers contains an ‚Äúerror apparent on the face of the record‚ÄĚ warranting review, 
particularly in light of contrary observations by a coordinate bench in PVVNL/Raman Ispat. 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Standards for Review (Article 137; Order XLVII CPC) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A review lies only where: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
There is an error apparent on the face of the record; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Something so obvious that it does not require elaborate argument; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Review cannot be used to re-argue or appeal the merits. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Doctrine of Coordinate Bench Discipline 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A bench of equal strength cannot comment upon, overrule, or criticise another coordinate bench. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč If a bench disagrees, it must refer the matter to a larger bench, failing which the law becomes uncertain. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On the Ground of ‚ÄúCoordinate Bench Comments‚ÄĚ ‚Äď rejected this argument outright: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Observations of a coordinate bench (PVVNL/Raman Ispat) cannot be grounds for review. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A bench of equal strength cannot undermine a previous coordinate bench. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
If PVVNL disagreed with Rainbow Papers, it should have referred the matter to a larger bench ‚Üí 
disagreement cannot generate an ‚Äúerror apparent.‚ÄĚ 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Did Rainbow Papers ‚Äúignore‚ÄĚ Section 53? ‚Äď factually incorrect. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Rainbow Papers reproduced Section 53 in full + discussed other IBC sections & Regulations (2016 & 2018) + 
considered Ghanashyam Mishra, Ebix, and earlier Supreme Court precedents ‚Üí argument is demonstrably false; 
hence no error apparent. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On the Classification of the State as a ‚ÄúSecured Creditor‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court reaffirms Rainbow Papers: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 3(30): ‚Äúsecured creditor‚ÄĚ includes creditor with security interest created by operation of law 
AND, GVAT s.48 creates such a charge. 
76 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
IBC does not exclude the government from being a secured creditor. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 53‚Äôs waterfall is not violated, because s.48 is not inconsistent with s.53. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, there is no legal error, let alone a ‚Äúmanifest error.‚ÄĚ 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Purpose of Review Not Satisfied 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Review is not a platform to reargue or seek reconsideration. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Petitioners are essentially re-litigating Rainbow Papers. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No patent error or oversight is demonstrated. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, the petition must be dismissed. 
 
Garodia Chemicals Limited [2025 | NCLAT] 
 
Facts: 
CD, a listed company with almost no business activity and acknowledging debt of only ‚āĻ4.41 lakh (mostly to a related party), filed for 
PPIRP under Section 54A(2)(g). Its sole external financial creditor, A (owed ‚āĻ9 lakh and holding 100% CoC voting share), had its 
director jointly submit the Base Resolution Plan, which A then approved. A had also paid the CD‚Äôs listing and NSDL fees just before 
filing. The arrangement appeared designed to transfer control of the listed company to the A group without triggering SEBI Takeover 
Regulations. The NCLT held that the PPIRP was being misused to bypass the takeover code rather than to resolve genuine 
insolvency. 
 
Issues: Whether a PPIRP application under Section 54C can be admitted when the filing appears motivated not by genuine 
insolvency resolution, but by an attempt to transfer control of a listed MSME and circumvent SEBI‚Äôs Takeover Regulations? 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 54A‚Äď54E (PPIRP Framework) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Applies only to MSMEs (s.54A(1)). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Filing permitted by corporate debtor with 66% financial creditor approval (s.54A(2), 54A(3)). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Debtor-in-possession model ‚Üí PPIRP relies on trust and genuine resolution intent. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč PPIRP is designed as a fast, promoter-friendly rescue mechanism for honest distressed MSMEs. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Doctrine of ‚Äúmisuse of insolvency process‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Insolvency proceedings cannot be used for collateral purposes such as: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
avoiding regulatory obligations, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
acquiring control, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
forum shopping, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
fraud or malicious intent. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLT must refuse admission where the purpose is not resolution. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč SEBI Takeover Code (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers Regulations, 2011) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Acquisition crossing specified thresholds triggers open offer obligations. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Regulatory obligations cannot be bypassed through IBC processes. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Genuine intent for PPIRP? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CD had no business for 3 years, negligible debt, and majority owed to related parties ‚Üí economic distress not 
demonstrated; no attempts at revival except BRP that transfers control ‚Üí suggests lack of genuine insolvency 
distress. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Conduct of A shows control-acquisition motive as it 
‚ěĘ‚Äč is the only voting financial creditor (100% voting share). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč paid listing fees and NSDL charges right before filing ‚Üí indicates plan to keep CD‚Äôs listed status alive. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč its director is a joint Resolution Applicant under the BRP. 
77 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The BRP would transfer control of the listed CD to A ‚Üí aligns with an acquisition strategy, not a resolution 
strategy. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Attempt to circumvent SEBI Takeover Code 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Direct acquisition of shareholding in a listed entity by A would invoke Takeover Regulations BUT, PPIRP route 
would allow it to bypass: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
open offer obligations, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
public shareholder protections, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
triggering thresholds. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLT concluded this was regulatory evasion, not insolvency resolution. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Legislative intent of PPIRP violated 
‚ěĘ‚Äč PPIRP is: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
meant for MSMEs, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
premised on good faith, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
intended to support honest promoters and maintain debtor-in-possession control. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč HERE, PPIRP is used to oust the existing promoter group and replace them with A. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The process is being used as a corporate control-transfer mechanism ‚Üí PPIRP cannot be used ‚Äúin the 
garb of insolvency resolution‚ÄĚ for such objectives. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT‚Äôs standard of review 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLT digs into motive and purpose behind filing. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Finds fraudulent intent: the PPIRP filed not for resolution but for control acquisition. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hence, application must be rejected at the pre-admission stage. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The PPIRP application under s.54C cannot be admitted. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč It is not intended for resolution, but is: ‚Äúan attempt to circumvent SEBI Takeover Regulations and engineer a transfer of 
control of a listed entity through PPIRP.‚ÄĚ 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The tribunal holds the PPIRP is tainted with fraudulent considerations. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Application dismissed. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT will not admit PPIRP applications where: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč insolvency is not genuine, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč the process is used to acquire corporate control, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SEBI regulations are being evaded, or 
‚ěĘ‚Äč PPIRP‚Äôs pro-MSME, promoter-trust model is being misused. 
 
 
78 
 
Competition Law
cases:
Emerging issues in corporate insolvency law 
STO v Rainbow Papers
key_principles: ‚Äč In addition to private creditors, the CD owed substantial statutory dues to the State under the GVAT Act (unpaid taxes) ‚Üí
‚Äč BUT, RP rejected/waived off the government‚Äôs claim ‚Üí treated it as an OC with no security interest.
‚Äč The approved Resolution Plan gave zero allocation to government dues.
‚Äč Government‚Äôs Argument ‚Üí under Section 48, Gujarat VAT Act:
‚Äč NCLT ‚Äď Held in favour of the government. Recognised charge under Section 48 GVAT as creating security interest.
‚Äč NCLAT ‚Äď set aside NCLT.
‚Äč Section 53 IBC overrides Section 48 GVAT ‚Üí gov is merely an operational creditor, placed low in waterfall
‚Äč Are the IBC timelines for submission of claims mandatory or directory?
‚Äč Does Section 48 of the GVAT Act create a ‚Äúsecurity interest‚ÄĚ, making the State a secured creditor under s.3(30), s.3(31) and
‚Äč Was the AA (NCLT) right in approving a Resolution Plan that gave zero dues to the Government?
‚Äč s.3(30) ‚ÄĒ Secured Creditor in whose favour security interest is created ‚Üí no exclusion of government.
‚Äč s.3(31) ‚ÄĒ Security Interest
‚Äč Includes mortgage, charge, hypothecation, assignment, encumbrance.
‚Äč Includes security interest created by operation of law.
‚Äč Excludes only performance guarantees.
‚Äč s.53(1)(b)(ii) ‚ÄĒ Waterfall
‚Äč Dues of secured creditors (who relinquished security interest).
‚Äč Rank pari passu with workmen‚Äôs dues for preceding 24 months.
‚Äč s.30(2)(b) ‚ÄĒ Mandatory contents of resolution plan
‚Äč Must provide at least liquidation value to operational creditors.
